name: Sensing regime orchestration across low-burden and full PSG nights
decision_axis: This lever controls data collection resolution by choosing between low-burden passive sensing, scheduled enhanced nights, and escalation full PSG.
consequences: Adopting a higher-resolution schedule increases data richness and event capture probability on study nights, but raises staffing demand, participant burden, and cost. A more intensive regime may also raise false-alarm rates if annotation throughput cannot keep pace, driving longer review cycles and potential data backlogs.
options: Rebalance toward continuous low-burden sensing using dry-electrode headbands, mattress sensors, and ambient cameras to maximize participant comfort and sustain longer stays.
Institute scheduled enhanced nights with full PSG on the first night and mid-stay night, while keeping low-burden monitoring on other days to optimize data richness with manageable workload.
Trigger escalation nights only after technician-flagged events with a rapid-response protocol, ensuring full PSG capture occurs when initial observations fail to explain the data.
review: Core tension: maximizing data resolution while preserving participant comfort and acceptable workload. A weakness: the options assume staffing can scale to support intensive nights without specifying budget or regulatory constraints for increased nocturnal workload.

name: Recruitment strategy calibration under safety and diversity goals
decision_axis: This lever controls recruitment strategy by choosing between strict eligibility criteria with narrow inclusion, broader criteria with enhanced monitoring, and quota-based stratified sampling.
consequences: Strict eligibility improves data consistency but slows enrollment and may limit generalizability. Broad eligibility accelerates enrollment and increases diversity but requires stronger safety monitoring to maintain participant safety. Stratified sampling ensures demographic balance but adds administrative complexity and potential delays in recruitment milestones.
options: Adopt strict eligibility focusing on well-characterized cases with robust collateral history, raising screening burden to improve data quality.
Adopt broad eligibility to maximize enrollment diversity while deploying standardized safety checklists and remote pre-screening to maintain risk control.
Implement stratified sampling quotas to balance age, sex, and parasomnia subtype while maintaining a consistent consent and screening cadence across sites.
review: Core tension: inclusivity vs data integrity. The options miss explicit mitigation for retention attrition and potential geographic biases limiting Bonn-based participation.

name: Data governance and privacy architecture
decision_axis: This lever controls data access by choosing between highly restricted internal access, tiered de-identified data sharing under data-use agreements, and broad external sharing under governance oversight.
consequences: Restricting access preserves participant privacy and reduces data leakage risk, but may limit external collaboration and validation opportunities. Tiered de-identified sharing expands research reach while maintaining privacy, potentially increasing citation and reproducibility, but may require substantial governance overhead and data-use agreements. Broad external sharing accelerates science but raises privacy risk and may trigger regulatory scrutiny; implementation relies on robust audit and redaction processes.
options: Enforce on-site data storage with stringent access controls, ensuring only essential personnel can view video and encryption is maintained for all transfers.

Establish tiered data access with de-identified physiological streams available to researchers under formal data-use agreements, while retaining video within secure, limited access for governance reviews.
Provide de-identified public datasets with automated redaction for any potential video-derived cues, subject to ongoing privacy audits and external governance checks.
review: Core tension: privacy versus openness. The options miss explicit consideration of cross-border collaborations or vendor risk in data handling.

name: Nocturnal staffing model and coverage plan
decision_axis: This lever controls staffing strategy by choosing between a lean core with cross-trained technicians, full-night rotation with standby responders, and selective contracting during peak periods.
consequences: Shifting toward a lean core reduces fixed costs and simplifies scheduling but increases fatigue risk and limits rapid response capacity. A full-night rotation enhances safety coverage and rapid event response, yet raises labor costs and potential burnout unless workload is managed. Seasonal or peak-period contracting improves flexibility and cost control but may introduce variability in staffing quality and training consistency.
options: Staff with a lean core team of 3 night technicians with cross-trained responsibilities, sustaining safety margins through automated alerts and clear escalation protocols.
Maintain full-night rotation of 4 technicians across eight suites plus a standby on-call responder for emergencies to minimize false alarm risk.
Contract out selective night coverage to certified contract sleep technologists during peak recruitment periods to preserve operational flexibility while controlling fixed costs.
review: Core tension: safety and workload versus cost containment. The options do not explicitly address nurse burnout, regulatory staffing limits, or training standardization across contractors.

name: Event triage workflow and scoring cadence
decision_axis: This lever controls annotation throughput by choosing between real-time dual scoring, asynchronous single-plus-second scoring, and semi-automated triage with human-in-the-loop.
consequences: Real-time dual scoring maximizes reliability and immediate feedback but ties up human resources and slows processing during busy periods. Asynchronous single-plus-second scoring improves throughput but may temporarily compromise cross-checking and consistency across raters. Semi-automated triage with human-in-the-loop speeds initial pass-through while preserving accuracy through subsequent human review, balancing efficiency and quality.
options: Maintain real-time scoring by dual raters on all nights to maximize reliability at the cost of longer processing times.
Adopt asynchronous scoring with scheduled rounds where a single rater performs initial scoring, followed by independent second scoring within 48 hours.
Implement a semi-automated triage system that flags likely events for rapid human review while batch-processing routine nights for efficiency.
review: Core tension: throughput versus accuracy. The options do not address calibration of scoring criteria or inter-rater training cadence.

name: Scale-up readiness and phased expansion criteria
decision_axis: This lever controls scale-up timing by choosing between go/no-go after milestones, phased expansion, and postponement with reallocation.
consequences: Choosing a go/no-go after milestones anchors expansion to demonstrated progress, reducing risk but potentially delaying impact. A phased expansion adds flexibility and learning, enabling protocol refinements but increasing administrative overhead and extending timelines. Postponement with reallocating funds preserves budget discipline but risks missing opportunities to capture early data quality advantages.
options: Proceed to full-scale expansion only after meeting predefined safety, data quality, and recruitment milestones with independent verification.
Implement phased expansion across two additional suites with interim evaluation points to refine protocols before committing further capital.
Delay expansion and redeploy funds to strengthen data processing and quality control measures, building a more robust evidence base before scaling.
review: Core tension: speed to scale versus assurance of quality and safety. The options do not specify contingency planning for regulatory or funding disruptions.

name: Consent scaffolding for longitudinal sleep data streams
decision_axis: This lever controls data-use scope for the resident data by choosing between broad unrestricted data sharing, restricted de-identified sharing for research, and on-site use only.
consequences: Enabling broader data-use increases research utility and potential cross-study comparisons, but expands privacy risk and governance overhead. It may require more frequent consent management and ethics reviews, potentially slowing downstream analyses and delaying data deposition in repositories.
options: Institute a tiered consent framework allowing broad data use only after re-consent and ethics review, with detailed audit trails.
Limit data to de-identified datasets accessible through secure university-controlled repositories with annual privacy impact assessments and controlled-down access.
Preserve privacy by restricting all data to local storage with no external sharing unless approved in governance, while enabling retrospective anonymized analyses.
review: Core tension: maximizing research utility while preserving participant privacy, particularly for video data. Weakness: none of the options explicitly enumerate the cost and resource implications of ongoing consent management and re-consent workflows across years.

name: Nightly event-triage escalation protocol design
decision_axis: This lever controls triage approach by choosing between clinician-led prioritization, multi-sensor corroboration with automated flagging, and strictly manual review with tiered severity thresholds.
consequences: Adopting clinician-led triage preserves gold-standard labeling but increases turnaround time during busy nights and elevates clinician workload. Multi-sensor corroboration with automated flagging can accelerate adjudication and stabilize consistency, yet raises dependence on sensor fidelity and requires maintenance of cross-disciplinary review processes. Manual-first thresholds reduce automation reliance but may slow throughput when event volumes surge.
options: Institute clinician-led triage with direct manual review on all flagged nights to ensure clinical judgment dominates labeling.
Adopt multi-sensor corroboration with automated flagging that prioritizes high-confidence events for rapid adjudication while deferring uncertain cases.
Implement a staged manual-first review for a subset of nights using severity thresholds and rotating clinician coverage to balance workload.
review: Core tension: maintaining high event accuracy under variable night volumes while avoiding clinician burnout; weakness: the options do not specify expected turnaround times or backlog risk during peak recruitment.

name: Tiered sensing architecture deployment plan
decision_axis: This lever controls sensor deployment trajectory by choosing between full PSG nights for all participants, low-burden monitoring with escalation to PSG on flagged nights, and alternating night intensities across participants.
consequences: Full PSG nights maximize data depth and event characterization but require substantial staffing and equipment use, increasing cost pressure. Low-burden monitoring reduces resource demand and sustains longer recruitment, but relies on escalation triggers that may miss subtle events and extend adjudication timelines. Alternating night intensities offers a middle path that preserves some data richness while smoothing staffing loads, at the expense of potential heterogeneity across participant data streams.
options: Implement full PSG nights for every participant on fixed schedule to maximize data richness and diagnostic validity at higher resource cost.
Operate low-burden monitoring for all participants with escalation to PSG only when sensor anomalies or clinical flags trigger review.
Adopt alternating night intensities across participants, balancing data depth with staffing constraints and reducing overnight fatigue.
review: Core tension: data richness vs staffing cost; weakness: the options do not quantify false-positive rates or time-to-resolution across tiers.

name: Resident privacy boundary management for live video streams
decision_axis: This lever controls live-video privacy boundaries by choosing between restricted local viewing with on-site staff, university-controlled access with audit logging, and fully anonymized aggregated video for research transcripts.
consequences: Restricting live viewing to on-site staff minimizes exposure but may hinder external collaboration and independent verification of events. Providing university-controlled access with audit trails increases collaboration potential while raising complexity and ongoing governance requirements. Anonymized aggregation preserves privacy and enables broad data use but limits how video-derived context can be leveraged for adjudication and methodological validation.
options: Limit live video viewing to on-site staff with stringent access controls and real-time audit logging to reduce exposure risk.
Enable university-controlled access to de-identified video data with automated masking and periodic privacy-impact assessments.
Publish fully anonymized aggregated video-derived metrics for research while preserving raw footage with restricted access.
review: Core tension: safety monitoring vs privacy; weakness: the options do not address regulatory drift or consent for video retention beyond current protocol.

name: Operational staffing model resilience and night-shift coverage
decision_axis: This lever controls coverage pattern by choosing between eight active suites with a nine-person core team, cross-trained staffing for flexible night shifts, and external on-call professionals to supplement peak demand.
consequences: Maintaining a fixed eight-suite arrangement with nine staff provides robust safety and data quality but constrains scalability and increases fixed costs. Cross-training staff offers flexibility and better coverage during irregular events, potentially reducing idle capacity but complicating scheduling and competency maintenance. External on-call professionals can scale up throughput during high-demand periods, at the cost of higher per-shift rates and coordination overhead.
options: Maintain eight active suites with nine-person core team, ensuring robust coverage, consistent data quality, and clear escalation paths.
Restructure staffing to cross-train technicians and researchers for flexible night shifts, reducing idle capacity while preserving safety.
Contract external on-call professionals to supplement peak demand periods, accepting higher per-shift costs to maintain throughput.
review: Core tension: staffing reliability vs cost; weakness: options don't quantify overnight fatigue or burnout risk.

name: Publications and data-annotation governance for open-access outputs
decision_axis: This lever controls dissemination strategy by choosing between rapid internal scoring for publication, staged external release with partner review, and embargoed data release with strict data-use agreements.
consequences: Rapid internal scoring accelerates early dissemination but may constrain external validation and data sharing timelines. Staged external release with partner review fosters collaboration while extending the window before public data access. Embargoed release with strict data-use agreements improves privacy protection and governance but can slow scientific impact and external replication efforts.
options: Prioritize rapid internal scoring and manuscript preparation to accelerate peer-reviewed publication while keeping datasets under restricted access until acceptance.
Institute staged external release with partner review and formal data-use agreements to enable broader collaboration without compromising privacy.
Adopt embargoed public-release of de-identified datasets only after rigorous governance review and long-term archiving in a recognized repository.
review: Core tension: speed of dissemination vs data privacy; weakness: the options may underestimate time required for ethical approvals or data-use agreement delays.

name: Night shift safety staffing cadence optimization
decision_axis: This lever controls staffing cadence by choosing between continuous on-site coverage, two 12-hour on-site shifts with on-call escalation, and remote monitoring with on-demand on-site visits.
consequences: Pulling this lever directly shapes how quickly the team can respond to events and the overall nightly safety coverage. Continuous on-site coverage reduces response time and improves perceived safety but increases fixed labor costs, while the two-shift model lowers expenses at the risk of fatigue and slower coverage during off-peak hours. Remote monitoring can cut labor spends but may introduce longer first-response times if alerts require on-site verification.
options: Maintain continuous on-site coverage with rotating teams to ensure immediate response and maintain safety margins, though this raises fixed labor costs.
Adopt two 12-hour on-site shifts with on-call escalation, balancing coverage and budget while relying on rapid remote decision-making to triage events.
Implement remote monitoring with on-demand on-site visits triggered by alert signals, prioritizing cost efficiency but potentially slowing first-response times.
review: Core tension: safety guarantees vs staffing cost and burnout; weakness: the options assume uniform incident frequency and do not account for regulatory overtime limits or seasonal variation.

name: Video privacy governance on live feed usage
decision_axis: This lever controls privacy posture by choosing between full masking and restricted access video, masked-only video with audit trails for research use, and no video data shared beyond the on-site team.
consequences: Implementing stricter privacy reduces risk of re-identification and regulatory leakage but may diminish data richness for event triage. Balancing access increases research utility but raises governance overhead and potential partner hesitancy. Relaxing privacy can improve data utility yet heighten privacy concerns and consent management burden.
options: Redact and blur all video streams with access limited to governance-approved reviewers, preserving safety data while protecting privacy.
Store video locally with strict audit trails and share only de-identified motion summaries with external researchers.
Omit video streams from any data sharing, relying entirely on sensor-based proxies for event triage.
review: Core tension: privacy preservation vs data utility; weakness: options may not address consent fatigue or partner requirements for video data.

name: Data annotation throughput and scorer reliability protocol
decision_axis: This lever controls scoring workflow by choosing between dual-independent scoring with adjudication, single scorer with periodic cross-checks, and a hybrid human-in-the-loop assisted by lightweight machine-assisted annotations.
consequences: This lever directly affects annotation reliability and downstream analysis throughput. Dual-independent scoring improves reliability but slows delivery; single scorers increase speed but risk inconsistency without calibration. A hybrid approach can balance accuracy and throughput yet requires robust tooling and clear adjudication protocols.
options: Engage two independent raters per event with formal adjudication to maximize reliability, accepting slower throughput.
Rely on a single trained scorer with periodic cross-checks and recalibration to accelerate review overall throughput.
Implement a hybrid approach where initial automated triage flags potential events for rapid human review, plus occasional external audits.
review: Core tension: reliability vs throughput; weakness: the options do not specify how to handle low inter-rater agreement or calibration cadence.

name: PSG-capable nights equipment upgrade path
decision_axis: This lever controls equipment upgrades by choosing between routine incremental PSG upgrades across the cohort, universal deployment of full PSG across all enhanced nights, and selective upgrades based on observed event density.
consequences: Upgrading equipment affects data resolution and staff workload. Routine incremental upgrades maintain balance but may slow quality gains; universal PSG maximizes data richness but increases staffing demands and costs; selective upgrades focus on high-yield periods but may create uneven data continuity.
options: Implement routine incremental PSG upgrades on a rolling subset of nights and participants to balance data resolution with budget.
Deploy full PSG on every enhanced night for all participants to maximize data quality, acknowledging higher staffing and cost.
Apply selective upgrades driven by prior event counts, escalating to full PSG only after a predefined event-density threshold is met.
review: Core tension: data quality vs resource strain; weakness: expansion could overwhelm the annotation pipeline if recruitment grows faster than data processing capacity.

name: Recruitment footprint optimization
decision_axis: This lever controls recruitment reach by choosing between Bonn-only enrollment, regional NRW-wide outreach, and national multi-site recruitment.
consequences: Broad recruitment expands eligibility and improves generalizability but adds coordination complexity and regulatory overhead; local Bonn-only enrollment simplifies logistics but may limit demographic diversity. Regional outreach can balance scope and manageability while maintaining operational control.
options: Concentrate recruitment within Bonn to streamline logistics and ensure consistent patient flow from the local population.
Expand outreach across the North Rhine-Westphalia region to increase eligibility, diversify demographics, and shorten recruitment timelines.
Launch national multi-site recruitment partnerships to maximize enrollment potential, spreading operational risk and leveraging broader clinical networks.
review: Core tension: depth of enrollment vs operational burden; weakness: national recruitment adds variability in consent processes and regulatory approvals across sites.

name: Public data sharing policy evolution
decision_axis: This lever controls data-sharing policy by choosing between fully de-identified datasets with controlled access, de-identified datasets with broader access under data-use agreements, and restricted to internal use only.
consequences: Opening data accelerates scientific discovery but raises privacy and governance obligations. Narrow sharing preserves privacy but may limit external validation and impact. A staged approach can optimize transparency while maintaining safeguards.
options: Provide fully de-identified datasets with controlled access to approved researchers under data-use agreements and periodic usage audits.
Offer de-identified datasets to a broader scientific audience under standardized data-use agreements, balancing openness with governance oversight.
Restrict data sharing to internal analysis only, publishing findings while withholding external data access to minimize privacy risk.
review: Core tension: openness vs privacy; weakness: even de-identified data may risk re-identification in rare scenarios if linked with external datasets.

name: Algorithm development governance and validation framework
decision_axis: This lever controls algorithm governance by choosing between external peer-reviewed validation, internal cross-validation with retrospective data, and staged open-source release with guarded access.
consequences: Validation framework shapes credibility, reproducibility, and clinical trust. External validation can accelerate adoption but may delay milestones due to reviewer timelines; internal validation provides speed but risks perceived independence. Open-source staging with safeguards encourages collaboration yet requires clear risk management.
options: Subject the algorithm to external peer-reviewed validation before deployment to ensure credibility and regulatory alignment.
Proceed with internal cross-validation using retrospective labeled data, then pilot deployment in a controlled subset to monitor performance.
Release the algorithm into a staged open-source environment with guarded access for contribution while preserving core clinical safeguards.
review: Core tension: external validation vs internal control; weakness: external validation may stall progress if access to reviewers is constrained.
